summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his August 2006 financial results for Bingo Card Creator, his first month of detailed public accounting. Despite expecting a small profit, he discovers a $1.90 loss after writing everything down, though cash flow remains positive due to timing differences in expense recognition. He breaks down sales ($333.54 net from 14 sales), expenses ($335.44 including advertising, hosting, and one-time costs), and detailed advertising and website traffic statistics. The post demonstrates the granular economics of a one-person micro-ISV in its earliest days.
key_points: First full month of business yielded a near-breakeven result: $333.54 net sales against $335.44 in expenses, a $1.90 loss
Google AdWords was the largest recurring expense at $90/month, with continuous optimization of ad text, landing pages, and keyword culling driving CPA toward his $0.30 target
A single teacher bulletin board post converted at 27%, far outperforming every paid channel — demonstrating the power of reaching your exact audience
The Long Tail applies to download sites: the top 5 sites account for roughly 40% of downloads, while the next 40 sites collectively contribute the remaining 60%
Yahoo Search Marketing was canceled due to untrustworthy analytics, consolidating spend on Google AdWords
money_quotes: Well, honestly, I thought I was going to post a minor paper profit. Hmm, I guess thats why we write things down.
The secret: continuous improvement of ad texts and landing pages, plus a recent ruthless culling of every keyword which wasn't performing well enough to justify keeping.
Single Post On Teacher Bulletin Board: 150 (27%) — I love my adoring fans.
Incidentally, the next 40 download sites on the list sum to about 60% of the total of these. Yep, Long Tail in action.
Basically it ended up being $30 given to charity for PR value.
I'm sorry to give you analog accuracy in a digital world, but its the best I can do without spending too much time playing accountant.
key_insight: Radical transparency about the economics of a micro-business reveals that even near-breakeven months contain valuable lessons about channel optimization, conversion tracking, and the importance of actually writing down your numbers.
